serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of uncooked maize grain is approximately 100 g, equivalent to roughly half a cup of dried kernels before cooking or grinding.

nutrient_source.title: Maize, yellow — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for uncooked yellow maize grain. All core macros and micronutrients sourced from this record. Saturated fat not reported by HPB; value estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for corn grain, yellow (FDC ID 170288) as 0.2 g/100g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 52 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweet corn / maize (boiled), which is the closest approved analogous food. Uncooked dried maize grain is not directly tested; the GI of 52 (low category) is applied as a conservative estimate for the raw grain form. Glycemic load per 100g serving = (52 × 73.3) / 100 = 38.1 (high GL at full 100g serving, reflecting the high carbohydrate density of dried grain).

hypertension.key_factor: Zero sodium and high potassium (428 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Uncooked maize is sodium-free with high potassium content (428 mg/100g), making it well-aligned with DASH dietary principles; avoid adding salt during cooking to preserve this benefit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate density (73.3 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (2.7 g/100g) producing high glycaemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: Uncooked maize has a very high carbohydrate density (73.3 g/100g) and low fibre (2.7 g/100g), resulting in a high glycaemic load per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion sizes, pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables, and prefer whole corn on the cob over dried grain to reduce postprandial glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.2 g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero sodium, and meaningful potassium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Uncooked maize is very low in saturated fat (0.2 g/100g), contains no cholesterol, and is sodium-free, supporting a heart-healthy dietary pattern; however, the high carbohydrate density warrants portion control to avoid excess caloric intake and triglyceride elevation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High glycaemic load promotes pro-inflammatory mediators; absence of meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Uncooked maize has a high glycaemic load and lacks anti-inflammatory nutrients (no omega-3, minimal antioxidants, insufficient fibre); individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit intake and pair with anti-inflammatory foods such as leafy greens, olive oil, or fatty fish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Maize (uncooked) refers to dried yellow maize kernels in their raw, unprocessed grain form, as consumed before cooking, grinding, or further processing. Also known as corn grain or dried corn, it is a globally cultivated cereal staple originating from the Americas and widely used across Africa, Asia, Latin America, and beyond. The kernels are firm, starchy, and have a mild, slightly sweet corn flavour in their raw state. Nutritionally, uncooked maize is energy-dense at 349 kcal per 100g, driven by a high carbohydrate content of 73.3 g/100g, predominantly complex starch. Protein is moderate at 8.1 g/100g and fat is low at 1.3 g/100g with minimal saturated fat (0.2 g/100g). Dietary fibre is 2.7 g/100g, which is insufficient to meaningfully blunt the glycaemic response of the dense starch load. Sodium is zero and potassium is notably high at 428 mg/100g, making it favourable for blood pressure management. Cholesterol is absent. Clinically, the high carbohydrate density and low fibre content produce a high glycaemic load per serving, making portion control essential for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. For cardiovascular health, the low saturated fat and zero sodium profile are positive attributes. For rheumatoid arthritis, the high glycaemic load is a concern. Uncooked maize is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal. It is used as a base for cornmeal, polenta, tortillas, ugali, and various traditional porridges worldwide.
common_user_queries: How many calories in uncooked corn?
Is dried maize high in carbs?
Nutritional value of raw maize grain
Is uncooked corn high in fibre?
Can I eat maize with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (primary approved regional source) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170288) as a secondary approved source. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for sweet corn/maize (boiled), clearly documented as an analogous estimate.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — uncooked dried maize grain contains negligible free sugars, with carbohydrate present almost entirely as starch; confirmed zero per HPB Foodo. sodium_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source. cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per HPB source — maize is a plant food and naturally contains no cholesterol. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for raw corn grain. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific record; detailed vitamin and mineral panel not available in the HPB source as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170288) does report many of these values and may be used for supplementary enrichment in a future pass. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entry for sweet corn/maize (boiled); no direct GI measurement for uncooked dried maize grain is available in the approved GI source. Glycaemic load of 38.1 per 100g serving is high, reflecting the dense carbohydrate content of dried grain; in practice, a cooked serving would be smaller and the GL correspondingly lower. This record applies to plain uncooked dried maize grain only; processed maize products (cornflour, cornmeal, cornflakes, popcorn, corn chips) have different nutritional profiles and require separate records.
